aliases: Chendol pandan mung bean jelly dessert

english: Chendol pandan mung bean jelly dessert

cuisine: Singaporean / Malaysian / Indonesian — Peranakan and Malay Hawker Dessert
food_subgroup: Shaved ice dessert with pandan jelly strips, red kidney beans, coconut milk, and gula melaka palm sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Chendol as a prepared composite dessert. Closest parent would be a shaved-ice dessert with legumes and coconut milk, which is not individually represented in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet dessert with legumes, coconut milk, and syrup (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Chendol. A0KLB used as closest defensible parent for a composite sweet dessert containing legumes, coconut milk, and sugar syrup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Chendol as a specific food item.

main_ingredients: pandan-flavoured mung bean flour jelly strips (cendol noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of Chendol weighs approximately 350 g and contains shaved ice (~200 g), pandan jelly strips (~50 g), cooked red kidney beans (~50 g), coconut milk (~30 g), and gula melaka syrup (~20 g), delivering approximately 357 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Chendol — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data with component cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central (coconut milk, FDC ID 169134)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central (secondary cross-reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chendol. Coconut milk fatty acid profile cross-referenced with USDA FDC 169134 (Coconut milk, raw). Trace minerals (copper, manganese) and vitamin K estimated from component analysis using USDA FDC data for individual ingredients where HPB data was not available; these fields carry confidence 0.9. All values expressed per 100 g of the composite as-served dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of palm sugar (gula melaka) and composite dessert estimation — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Wolever TMS et al. peer-reviewed GI methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chendol exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~55 (low) using a weighted composite approach: gula melaka (palm sugar) GI ~38 per University of Sydney GI Database (coconut/palm sugar entry); rice/mung bean starch jelly GI ~70–75 (analogous to rice starch-based products); red kidney beans GI ~28 per University of Sydney GI Database; coconut milk fat content moderates overall glycemic response. Weighted composite yields GI ~55. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (55 × 40 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.0. This is a low-GI, high-GL food due to large serving carbohydrate quantity. GI 55 is considered low per University of Sydney GI Database classification (55 or less is low).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~17 mg/100 g; ~60 mg per 350 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Chendol is one of the lowest-sodium Singapore hawker desserts (~60 mg sodium per bowl, well below the 600 mg per-meal threshold), making it acceptable for hypertension management from a sodium perspective; however, the high saturated fat content from coconut milk (lauric acid ~13 g per bowl) warrants moderation for overall cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total sugar load (~44 g per 350 g bowl) and medium-high glycemic load (GL ~22) despite gula melaka's lower GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chendol should be avoided or consumed only on rare special occasions by people with Type 2 Diabetes, as one bowl delivers approximately 44 g of sugar (140–160% of the WHO daily free-sugar limit) and a glycemic load of ~22; if consumed, request 'kurang manis' (less syrup) to reduce sugar by up to 40–50% and lower the GL to approximately 12–13.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~13 g per 350 g bowl, predominantly lauric acid C12:0) combined with high sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should limit Chendol to occasional consumption due to its high saturated fat content (~13 g per bowl, approximately 65% of the AHA daily SFA limit) from coconut milk lauric acid, which raises LDL cholesterol, compounded by a high sugar load that promotes triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance; requesting reduced coconut milk ('sedikit santan') can lower SFA by approximately 30–40%.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk saturated fat (lauric acid) may activate TLR4 pro-inflammatory pathways; high sugar load promotes AGE production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Chendol in moderation, as the high lauric acid content from coconut milk may activate TLR4-mediated pro-inflammatory pathways and the high sugar load promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation, both of which may exacerbate systemic inflammation with chronic exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chendol (also spelled Cendol; Chinese: 煎蕊 Jiān Ruǐ; Malay: Cendol; Tamil: சென்டோல்; Indonesian: Cendol) is a beloved Southeast Asian shaved-ice dessert served widely at Singapore hawker centres, Malaysian street food stalls, and Indonesian eateries. A standard bowl (~350 g) is assembled by layering cooked red kidney beans at the base, topping with finely shaved ice, then generously drizzling with rich coconut milk and thick gula melaka (dark palm sugar) syrup. The defining visual and textural element is the bright-green 'worm-like' jelly noodles — made from mung bean or rice flour mixed with pandan leaf extract — which are extruded through a perforated mould directly into the bowl. The result is a multi-textured dessert: icy and refreshing, creamy from the coconut milk, earthy from the beans, and intensely aromatic from the pandan and caramelised palm sugar. Nutritionally, one bowl provides approximately 357 kcal, 4.6 g protein, 15 g fat (13 g saturated, predominantly lauric acid from coconut milk), 56 g carbohydrate, and 44 g sugar. Sodium is very low (~60 mg per bowl). The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low) but the glycemic load is high (~22) due to the large quantity of gula melaka syrup. Despite gula melaka having a lower GI (~38) than refined sugar, the total sugar quantity makes Chendol a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes management — one bowl delivers 140–160% of the WHO daily free-sugar limit. The high saturated fat content (~65% of AHA daily SFA limit per bowl) is a cardiovascular concern. Chendol is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is culturally significant in Peranakan cuisine and is a popular afternoon snack or post-meal dessert throughout Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and key micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Coconut milk fatty acid profile cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC 169134). Trace minerals (copper, manganese) and vitamin K estimated from component analysis using USDA FDC data for individual ingredients where HPB data was not available; these fields carry confidence 0.9 but do not affect core macro integrity. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values, clearly documented. Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for select micronutrients.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite as-served Chendol dish (including shaved ice contribution) is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; the shaved ice component makes total water_g estimation unreliable without a dedicated moisture assay. Source consulted: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FDC 169134 — neither reports composite moisture for this preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA FDC 169134 coconut milk fatty acid profile scaled to the composite dish; these are approximate. 3. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Chendol preparation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based vegan dessert containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FDC component data. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — no vitamin D sources (animal fats, fortified dairy) are present in traditional Chendol; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. 6. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. 7. Copper (0.05 mg/100 g), manganese (0.12 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FDC component data for individual ingredients (coconut milk FDC 169134, red kidney beans FDC 173744, mung bean starch); confidence 0.9 for these fields. 8. GI value of 55 is a composite analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chendol exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 9. FoodEx2 code A0KLB is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Chendol. 10. Serving size set to 350 g as the midpoint of the documented hawker portion range (300–400 g); GL per serving calculated as (55 × 40 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 22.0. 11. GI category set to 'low' as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI 55 or less is low).
